title: HPC Architecture & Operations
description: Understand HPC cluster architecture, job scheduling with SLURM, parallel computing with MPI, storage systems, and benchmarking.

lessons.title: HPC Cluster Architecture
objectives: Identify the components of a modern HPC cluster
Understand head nodes, compute nodes, and interconnects
Describe GPU node architecture and NVLink topology
lessons.content_md:
# HPC Cluster Architecture

## Anatomy of an HPC Cluster

A modern AI/HPC cluster is a carefully engineered system. Here is the big picture:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    USERS (SSH)                          │
└───────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┘
                        │
┌───────────────────────▼─────────────────────────────────┐
│                  LOGIN / HEAD NODES                     │
│  (job submission, compilation, file management)         │
└───────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┘
                        │  Management Network (Ethernet)
┌───────────────────────▼─────────────────────────────────┐
│              RESOURCE MANAGER (SLURM)                   │
│  slurmctld (controller) + slurmd (on each node)        │
└──────┬──────────┬──────────┬──────────┬─────────────────┘
       │          │          │          │  High-Speed Fabric
┌──────▼───┐┌─────▼────┐┌───▼──────┐┌──▼───────┐
│ Compute  ││ Compute  ││ Compute  ││ Compute  │
│ Node 001 ││ Node 002 ││ Node 003 ││ Node ... │
│ 8x A100  ││ 8x A100  ││ 8x A100  ││ 8x A100 │
└──────┬───┘└─────┬────┘└───┬──────┘└──┬───────┘
       │          │          │          │  Storage Network
┌──────▼──────────▼──────────▼──────────▼───────────────┐
│            PARALLEL STORAGE (Lustre / GPFS)           │
│        High-bandwidth shared filesystem               │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

:::interactive{component="NetworkTopologyViewer"}

## GPU Node Architecture

A DGX-style GPU node (the workhorse of AI training):

| Component | Specification |
|-----------|--------------|
| GPUs | 8x NVIDIA A100/H100 (80GB each) |
| GPU Interconnect | NVLink 3.0/4.0 (600-900 GB/s per GPU) |
| CPUs | 2x AMD EPYC or Intel Xeon (64-128 cores) |
| RAM | 1-2 TB DDR5 |
| Local Storage | 4-8x NVMe SSDs (30 TB total) |
| Network | 8x InfiniBand HDR/NDR (200-400 Gb/s each) |

## Network Topology

The interconnect fabric is critical. Common topologies:

- **Fat-tree**: Equal bandwidth at every level. Most common in HPC.
- **Dragonfly**: Groups of switches connected with global links. Used in Cray systems.
- **Rail-optimized**: Each GPU connects to its own InfiniBand HCA for maximum bandwidth.

For 8-GPU nodes with rail-optimized topology:
```
GPU0 ──── IB HCA 0 ────┐
GPU1 ──── IB HCA 1 ────┤
GPU2 ──── IB HCA 2 ────┤──── Leaf Switches ──── Spine Switches
...                     │
GPU7 ──── IB HCA 7 ────┘
```

:::interactive{component="GPUMemoryVisualizer"}

exercises.title: Cluster Components
exercises.description: What is the primary role of login/head nodes in an HPC cluster?
options: Job submission, compilation, and file management
exercises.correct_answer: Job submission, compilation, and file management
hints: Users interact with the cluster through these nodes, but computation happens elsewhere
exercises.solution: Job submission, compilation, and file management

exercises.title: GPU Interconnect
exercises.description: What technology provides direct GPU-to-GPU communication within a single node?
hints: This is NVIDIA's proprietary high-bandwidth GPU interconnect

exercises.title: Cluster Inventory Report
exercises.description: Write a bash script that SSHs into each node in a list and reports the hostname, number of GPUs, total GPU memory, and CPU count.
hints: Use nvidia-smi for GPU info and nproc for CPU count
exercises.solution:
#!/bin/bash
for node in node-{001..004}; do
  echo "=== $node ==="
  ssh $node "hostname; nvidia-smi -L | wc -l; nvidia-smi --query-gpu=memory.total --format=csv,noheader; nproc"
done

lessons.title: Job Schedulers — SLURM Deep Dive
objectives: Write SLURM job scripts for GPU workloads
Use sbatch, srun, squeue, and scancel effectively
Configure partitions, job arrays, and dependencies
lessons.content_md:
# Job Schedulers — SLURM Deep Dive

SLURM (Simple Linux Utility for Resource Management) is the dominant job scheduler in HPC. It manages all compute resources and ensures fair allocation.

## Essential SLURM Commands

| Command | Purpose | Example |
|---------|---------|---------|
| `sbatch` | Submit batch job | `sbatch train.sh` |
| `srun` | Run interactive/parallel task | `srun --gpus=1 python test.py` |
| `squeue` | View job queue | `squeue -u $USER` |
| `scancel` | Cancel job | `scancel 12345` |
| `sinfo` | Cluster status | `sinfo -N -l` |
| `sacct` | Job accounting | `sacct -j 12345 --format=JobID,Elapsed,MaxRSS` |
| `scontrol` | Job/node control | `scontrol show job 12345` |

:::interactive{component="TerminalSimulator" config='{"preset":"slurm-commands"}'}

## Writing a SLURM Job Script

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=llm-training
#SBATCH --partition=gpu-a100
#SBATCH --nodes=4
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=8
#SBATCH --gpus-per-node=8
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=12
#SBATCH --mem=0                    # Use all available memory
#SBATCH --time=72:00:00            # 3-day time limit
#SBATCH --output=logs/%x_%j.out    # %x=job name, %j=job ID
#SBATCH --error=logs/%x_%j.err
#SBATCH --exclusive                # No sharing the node

# Load modules
module load cuda/12.2 nccl/2.19 python/3.11

# Set distributed training environment
export MASTER_ADDR=$(scontrol show hostnames $SLURM_JOB_NODELIST | head -n1)
export MASTER_PORT=29500
export WORLD_SIZE=$((SLURM_NNODES * SLURM_NTASKS_PER_NODE))

# Launch distributed training
srun torchrun \
    --nnodes=$SLURM_NNODES \
    --nproc_per_node=$SLURM_NTASKS_PER_NODE \
    --rdzv_id=$SLURM_JOB_ID \
    --rdzv_backend=c10d \
    --rdzv_endpoint=$MASTER_ADDR:$MASTER_PORT \
    train.py --config configs/llama_7b.yaml
```

## Job Arrays

Run many similar jobs with different parameters:

```bash
#SBATCH --array=0-9      # Creates 10 jobs (indices 0-9)

CONFIGS=(lr_0.001 lr_0.01 lr_0.1 bs_32 bs_64 bs_128 wd_0.01 wd_0.1 opt_adam opt_sgd)
CONFIG=${CONFIGS[$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID]}
python train.py --config "$CONFIG"
```

## Job Dependencies

Chain jobs together:

```bash
# Training job
JOB1=$(sbatch --parsable train.sh)

# Evaluation — runs after training completes
JOB2=$(sbatch --parsable --dependency=afterok:$JOB1 eval.sh)

# Report — runs after evaluation
sbatch --dependency=afterok:$JOB2 report.sh
```

:::interactive{component="ProcessSchedulerViz"}

hints: The 'per-node' suffix means the resource is requested for each node

Use $SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID to index into the array

lessons.title: Parallel Computing with MPI
objectives: Understand message passing and MPI communication patterns
Write basic MPI programs with mpi4py
lessons.content_md:
# Parallel Computing with MPI

MPI (Message Passing Interface) is the standard for distributed computing in HPC. While deep learning frameworks abstract away most MPI details, understanding MPI concepts helps you debug distributed training issues.

## Core Concepts

- **Communicator**: A group of processes that can communicate (MPI_COMM_WORLD = all processes)
- **Rank**: A process's unique ID within a communicator (0 to N-1)
- **Size**: Total number of processes

## mpi4py Example

```python
from mpi4py import MPI

comm = MPI.COMM_WORLD
rank = comm.Get_rank()
size = comm.Get_size()

print(f"Hello from rank {rank} of {size}")

# Point-to-point communication
if rank == 0:
    data = {"key": "value", "numbers": [1, 2, 3]}
    comm.send(data, dest=1, tag=42)
    print(f"Rank 0 sent data to rank 1")
elif rank == 1:
    data = comm.recv(source=0, tag=42)
    print(f"Rank 1 received: {data}")
```

Run with: `mpirun -np 4 python script.py`

:::interactive{component="TerminalSimulator" config='{"preset":"mpi-commands"}'}

## Collective Operations

| Operation | Description | AI Equivalent |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| Broadcast | One-to-all | Send model weights to all GPUs |
| Scatter | Split data to all | Distribute data batches |
| Gather | Collect from all | Collect predictions |
| Reduce | Combine with operation | Sum gradients |
| **AllReduce** | Reduce + broadcast | **Gradient synchronization** |
| AllGather | Gather + broadcast to all | Collect embeddings |

**AllReduce is the most important operation in distributed deep learning.** It sums gradients across all GPUs and distributes the result back.

```python
import numpy as np
from mpi4py import MPI

comm = MPI.COMM_WORLD
rank = comm.Get_rank()

# Each process has local gradients
local_gradient = np.random.randn(1000).astype(np.float32)

# AllReduce: sum all gradients and distribute result
global_gradient = np.zeros_like(local_gradient)
comm.Allreduce(local_gradient, global_gradient, op=MPI.SUM)

# Now every process has the sum of all gradients
global_gradient /= comm.Get_size()  # Average
```

:::interactive{component="CUDAVisualizer"}

exercises.title: AllReduce Purpose
exercises.description: Why is AllReduce the most critical MPI operation for distributed deep learning?
options: It synchronizes gradients across all GPUs and returns the result to all
exercises.correct_answer: It synchronizes gradients across all GPUs and returns the result to all
hints: After each training step, all GPUs need the combined gradients to update the model identically
exercises.solution: It synchronizes gradients across all GPUs and returns the result to all

lessons.title: HPC Storage Systems
objectives: Optimize I/O patterns for training workloads
lessons.content_md:
# HPC Storage Systems

Storage is often the biggest bottleneck in AI training. Understanding parallel filesystems helps you avoid I/O-bound training.

## Lustre Filesystem

Lustre is the most common HPC filesystem, used on most Top500 supercomputers.

```
┌─────────────┐
│ MDS (Metadata│  Handles file names, permissions, directory structure
│   Server)    │
└──────┬──────┘
       │
┌──────▼──────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐
│ OSS/OST #1  │  │ OSS/OST #2 │  │ OSS/OST #N │  Object Storage Servers
│ (stores data│  │            │  │            │  Each manages disk targets
│  chunks)    │  │            │  │            │
└─────────────┘  └────────────┘  └────────────┘
```

**Key concept: Striping** — A single file is split across multiple OSTs for parallel I/O.

```bash
# Set stripe count (spread file across 4 OSTs)
lfs setstripe -c 4 -S 4M /lustre/training_data/

# Check current striping
lfs getstripe /lustre/training_data/dataset.tar

# View filesystem usage
lfs df -h
```

:::interactive{component="TerminalSimulator" config='{"preset":"lustre-storage"}'}

## I/O Best Practices for AI Training

1. **Use large files, not millions of small ones** — Metadata operations are expensive
2. **Pre-process data into efficient formats**: WebDataset, TFRecord, HDF5
3. **Use local NVMe for hot data**: Copy datasets to node-local SSDs before training
4. **Stripe large files**: More stripes = more parallel bandwidth
5. **Avoid random small reads**: Sequential I/O is 10-100x faster

```bash
# Stage data to local NVMe before training
#SBATCH --tmp=500G    # Request local storage

cp /lustre/datasets/imagenet.tar $TMPDIR/
tar -xf $TMPDIR/imagenet.tar -C $TMPDIR/data/
python train.py --data-dir $TMPDIR/data/
```

:::interactive{component="PipelineBuilder" config='{"preset":"ml-training"}'}

exercises.title: Lustre Striping
exercises.description: What does increasing the stripe count of a file in Lustre achieve?
options: Spreads data across more servers for parallel I/O
exercises.correct_answer: Spreads data across more servers for parallel I/O
hints: Striping divides a file into chunks stored on different servers
exercises.solution: Spreads data across more servers for parallel I/O

lessons.title: Benchmarking & Performance
objectives: Run standard HPC benchmarks (HPL, HPCG, Stream)
Profile GPU workloads with nsys and ncu
lessons.content_md:
# Benchmarking & Performance

Benchmarking validates your cluster is performing as expected and helps identify bottlenecks.

## Standard Benchmarks

| Benchmark | Measures | Tool |
|-----------|----------|------|
| HPL | Peak FLOPS (dense linear algebra) | `xhpl` |
| HPCG | Memory-bound performance | `xhpcg` |
| STREAM | Memory bandwidth | `stream_c` |
| NCCL Tests | GPU-to-GPU communication | `nccl-tests` |
| MLPerf | Real ML training/inference | `mlperf` |

## NCCL Tests — Critical for AI

```bash
# AllReduce bandwidth test (most important for distributed training)
mpirun -np 8 --bind-to numa \
    ./build/all_reduce_perf \
    -b 1M -e 4G -f 2 -g 1

# Expected output for 8x A100 with NVLink:
# size    time    algbw    busbw
# 4GB     2.1ms   242GB/s  423GB/s
```

## GPU Profiling with NVIDIA Nsight

```bash
# Profile a training step
nsys profile --trace=cuda,nvtx,osrt \
    --output=training_profile \
    python train.py --steps=10

# View the profile
nsys-ui training_profile.nsys-rep

:::interactive{component="TerminalSimulator" config='{"preset":"gpu-profiling"}'}

# Detailed kernel analysis
ncu --set full \
    --target-processes all \
    python train.py --steps=1
```

## Common Bottlenecks

1. **GPU Utilization < 80%**: Data loading too slow — increase DataLoader workers, use local NVMe
2. **High AllReduce time**: Network bottleneck — check InfiniBand health, NCCL settings
3. **Memory OOM**: Reduce batch size, enable gradient checkpointing, use mixed precision
4. **Slow checkpointing**: Write to local NVMe first, then async-copy to shared storage

:::interactive{component="GPUMemoryVisualizer"}

exercises.title: Bottleneck Identification
exercises.description: If GPU utilization is at 40% during training, what is the most likely bottleneck?
options: The learning rate is too high
hints: Low GPU utilization means the GPUs are waiting for data

exercises.title: Benchmark Analysis Script
exercises.description: Write a Python script that parses NCCL test output (lines with size, time, algbw, busbw) and generates a summary showing min/max/avg bus bandwidth.
hints: NCCL test output has columns separated by whitespace
Bus bandwidth is typically the last column
Filter lines that start with a number (size in bytes)
exercises.solution:
import sys
bandwidths = []
for line in sys.stdin:
    parts = line.strip().split()
    if parts and parts[0].isdigit():
        busbw = float(parts[-1])
        bandwidths.append(busbw)
if bandwidths:
    print(f"Min: {min(bandwidths):.1f} GB/s")
    print(f"Max: {max(bandwidths):.1f} GB/s")
    print(f"Avg: {sum(bandwidths)/len(bandwidths):.1f} GB/s")
